Anish Kumar

Atmospheric Scientist · PhD in Meteorology

Location Leipzig, Germany
About

Research profile

My work combines atmospheric dynamics, numerical modelling, data analysis, and explainable machine learning to study variability, extremes, and predictability across stratospheric and tropospheric systems.

Thesis: “Advances in Understanding of Exceptionally Strong Arctic Stratospheric Polar Vortex Dynamics and Associated Ozone Loss.”

My PhD research at the Institute for Meteorology, Leipzig University focused on the variability, morphology, persistence, and extreme states of the Arctic stratospheric polar vortex using ERA5, observational datasets, ICON simulations, CMIP output, and related climate-model diagnostics.

In parallel, I developed explainable machine learning approaches for Arctic ozone prediction, combining statistical learning, SHAP-based interpretation, ensemble methods, and dynamical diagnostics.

Before my PhD, I worked extensively on WRF-based studies of severe convective storms, lightning, heavy rainfall, and hailstorm events over South Asia, including sensitivity experiments, data assimilation, forecast evaluation, and real-time modelling workflows.

2026 PhD in Meteorology
15+ Journal papers and thesis outputs
2 Core modelling systems: ICON & WRF

Research interests

Climate and Weather Extremes · Numerical Modeling · Explainable Machine Learning

My broader interests include stratospheric dynamics, ozone variability, severe weather, model evaluation, atmospheric predictability, and scientific computing for weather and climate applications.

Research

Research themes

My work spans large-scale atmospheric dynamics, severe weather modelling, and data-driven methods for understanding variability and predictability.

Stratospheric dynamics

Arctic stratospheric polar vortex variability, exceptionally strong vortex events, vortex morphology, persistence, large-scale circulation diagnostics, and links to climate variability.

Ozone variability and loss

Dynamical controls on Arctic ozone variability, vortex-related ozone loss, and coupled chemical-dynamical interpretation using observations, reanalysis, and model output.

Numerical modelling

ICON experiments on gravity-wave forcing and stratospheric circulation, WRF simulations of severe weather, sensitivity studies, data assimilation, and forecast evaluation.

Explainable machine learning

Regression, ensemble prediction, uncertainty assessment, and SHAP-based interpretation for physically consistent atmospheric prediction and diagnostic insight.

Weather and climate extremes

Severe storms, lightning, hailstorms, heavy rainfall, circulation extremes, predictability, and process-based evaluation of high-dimensional geophysical datasets.

Applied atmospheric science

Climate-tech, environmental prediction, energy and planning applications, climate-risk analysis, and trustworthy AI for Earth-system decision support.

Experience

Research and professional background

Research experience across stratospheric dynamics, severe weather modelling, atmospheric diagnostics, and scientific computing.

Jul 2023 – May 2026

Doctoral Researcher · Leipzig University, Germany

Investigated Arctic stratospheric polar vortex dynamics, ozone loss, ICON simulations, CMIP and ERA5 datasets, stratospheric aerosol intervention scenarios, and explainable machine learning frameworks for Arctic ozone prediction.

Sep 2021 – Jun 2023

Project Associate I · Central University of Rajasthan, India

Conducted WRF-based simulations of severe convective storms, lightning, heavy rainfall, and hailstorm events; performed sensitivity experiments, model validation, WRFDA/3DVAR data assimilation, and real-time forecast workflow development.

Jul 2019 – Jul 2021

MSc Atmospheric Science · Central University of Rajasthan, India

Developed a foundation in atmospheric dynamics, weather systems, numerical modelling, and data analysis for geophysical applications.

Publications

Selected publications

Selected peer-reviewed publications from my work on Arctic stratospheric dynamics, ozone variability, and numerical weather modelling.

Anish Kumar. “Advances in Understanding of Exceptionally Strong Arctic Stratospheric Polar Vortex Dynamics and Associated Ozone Loss.” PhD thesis, Leipzig University, 2026.

Doctoral thesis on extreme Arctic stratospheric vortex dynamics and associated ozone loss.

PhD thesis Repository

Anish Kumar, Khalil Karami, and Christoph Jacobi. “A 45-Year Climatological Study of Arctic Stratospheric Polar Vortex Dynamics and Morphology using ERA5 Data (1979–2023).” Climate Dynamics, 2025.

Long-term climatological analysis of Arctic stratospheric vortex dynamics and morphology using ERA5.

Climate Dynamics DOI

Anish Kumar, Khalil Karami, Christoph Jacobi, and Sina Mehrdad. “Exploring the impact of orographic and non-orographic gravity waves on Arctic stratospheric polar vortex dynamics and springtime ozone loss.” JASTP, 2025.

ICON-based investigation of gravity-wave forcing effects on stratospheric circulation and ozone loss.

JASTP DOI

Anish Kumar, Joyjit Mandal, Sina Mehrdad, and Christoph Jacobi. “A Novel Approach to Predict the Arctic Stratospheric Ozone from Stratospheric Polar Vortex Dynamics using Explainable Machine Learnings.” Scientific Reports, 2025.

Explainable machine learning framework connecting ozone prediction with polar vortex dynamics.

Scientific Reports DOI

Anish Kumar, S. K. Panda, Unashish Mondal, Devesh Sharma, and Someshwar Das. “Assessment of model time step and domain resolution of WRF model to simulate the lightning over Bihar and Rajasthan, India.” Modeling Earth Systems and Environment, 2023.

WRF sensitivity analysis showing how model time step and domain resolution influence lightning simulation over India.

Modeling Earth Systems and Environment DOI

Anish Kumar, Unashish Mondal, Subrat Kumar Panda, and Sakia Shabnam Kader. “Sensitivity of horizontal grid resolution on the simulation of thunderstorms over Bangladesh using Weather Research Forecasting Model.” Arabian Journal of Geosciences, 2024.

Numerical study of thunderstorm simulation sensitivity to horizontal grid resolution using the WRF model.

Arabian Journal of Geosciences DOI

Teaching, supervision, and mentoring

Experience in supporting student learning through teaching assistance, project mentoring, and co-supervision.

Teaching assistant roles

Student supervision

  • BSc thesis: Arctic stratospheric polar vortex and springtime ozone loss.
  • MSc thesis: Sensitivity experiments on WRF physical configuration for lightning events over Germany.
  • Seminar project: Tropospheric response to strong and stable Arctic stratospheric polar vortex events.

Academic contribution

My teaching and mentoring experience reflects a strong interest in communicating atmospheric science, guiding student projects, and supporting research training across both stratospheric and tropospheric topics.

Conferences

Conference presentations and workshops
